ADJ.etymology: From stor, from stórr, from *stōraz, from *stāro-.
From svār, svāger, svor, from svárr, from *swēraz, from *swer-. Compare schwer.
From viss, from *gawissaz. Cognate with viss.
From starker, from starkr, from *starkuz, from *sterg-.
vanaligr. Compare vanlig, vanalegur and vanleg.
Since at least 1621, from braf; from brav; from brave, borrowed from bravo.
From eiginn, from *aiganaz, past participle of *aiganą. Equivalent to -en. Cognate with own.
From rolig. Rolig, in the meaning ''calm'', is obsolete in contemporary Swedish so ''rolig'' is not considered an antonym of this word. (In contemporary Swedish, rolig means ''funny''.)
From swak, from *swak, from *swak. Compare with schwach. The form with -g- is due to - influence.
weldich, whence also vældig and veldig (which are usually used in a different way from the Swedish word). See the related gewaltig for more.
From dáligr, compare with dårlig, dårlig, dåleg.
From -. staka, variant form of stake.
From illa, from ílla, illa, adverb formation of ill, ilder, íllr, illr. Related to ill.
From kristin, from kristinn, from kristen or cristen, from Christianus, from χρῑστιᾱνός.
From manger, margher, marghin, from mangr, margr, from *managaz.
From næstr, a superlative of ná, corresponding to *nēhwist.
-lig. Note that the verb rimma is not only used in the literal sense "to rhyme" but also in the figurative sense "to fit".
From oddi, dative singular of oddr.

ADP.etymology: From ivir, yvir, ø̄wer, from Runic Swedish ufiR, from yfir, from *ubiri, from *upéri, from *upér, from *upo.

From som or sum, in Runic inscriptions also ''sim'', same as sem, from sem. Also related to the prefix sam- and suffix -sam. Still in the Poetic Edda, the Icelandic ''sem'' is only used as a comparative particle, e.g. Há
From siþan, from síðan. See Norwegian siden. Cognate with siþþan.

ADV.etymology: From hér, from *hē₂r.
Corresponding to och / ock + så, from ok svā, from ok and svá. Compare også.
From iū, io. Compare jo, jo, ja (adverb) / je (conjunction).
From þar, from *þar.
Shortened form of huru, from hwaru, from neuter dative of *hwar
From þa, from þá, from *þan.
From út, from *ūt.
From seinn.
weldich, whence also vældig and veldig (which are usually used in a different way from the Swedish word). See the related gewaltig for more.
From upp, from *upp.
From bara, from *bara, from *barr, from *bazaz. Compare bara.
From nær, from *nēhwiz, comparative of *nēhw. Ultimately from *h₂neḱ-.
From richtich or richtig. Compare rigtig.
From þær for, equivalent to för. Compare therefore, derfor, dafür.
Contraction of litet, neuter of liten. Compare mycket, with similar formation.

ig, similar to German ständig. The Swedish/German verb stå/stehen is the basis for the noun stånd/Stand, which can mean both upright standing (without support) and still standing (without movement or change). The still-s
From vel, from *wela, from *welh₁-. Common Germanic word. Cognate with vel, wel, well, væl, wohl, 𐍅𐌰𐌹𐌻𐌰, vel, vel, vel.
Borrowed from also. Compare allt and så. Cognate to altså and altso.

From tíðligr and tidelik, related to tid.
From aldri, aldrigi. Cognate with aldrei, aldri, aldri and aldrig.

From sízt.
räckalig
From ūte, from úti.
From even, effen, from evan, from *ebn, from *ebnaz, cognate with even, even. Compare effen. See also jämn.

hælder, from heldr.
From lengi.
From nóg, nógr, gnógr, from *ganōgaz. Ultimately from *h₂eh₂nóḱe, perfective of *h₂neḱ-.
From siþan, from síðan. See Norwegian siden. Cognate with siþþan.
From stor, from stórr, from *stōraz, from *stāro-.

From total, from total, from tōtālis.

From almennr. män. Compare allmenn.
From tid, literally "all time". Compare altid, altijd, ætíð, alltid, alltid.
Borrowed from an and an, and less commonly from on, from *ana, cognate with on and doublet of å, på.
From definitivus.

From utom.
From fram, from *fram.
From åt, also written as separate words, attested as such (''fram åt'') in the 1541 Swedish Bible translation (Ezekiel 1:12).

From svār, svāger, svor, from svárr, from *swēraz, from *swer-. Compare schwer.

From til baka, from til + baka, genitive plural of baker, equivalent to baks, according to SAOB online the alternative forms with -s are however not hence this fact, but rather from the genitive singular. Cognate with ti
From til saman, til samans, from tosamene. Cognate with tilsammen, til sammen, tilsammen, zusammen, tousommen, tosamen, tezamen and zesummen.

AUX.etymology: From koma, from koma, from *kwemaną, from *gʷem-. orgasm is probably a come.
a, cognate with behoove (US) / behove (UK) and behoeven.
From lēre, from lēra. Compare lære.

CCONJ.etymology: From som or sum, in Runic inscriptions also ''sim'', same as sem, from sem. Also related to the prefix sam- and suffix -sam. Still in the Poetic Edda, the Icelandic ''sem'' is only used as a comparative particle, e.g. Há

NOUN.etymology: From vrāge (Frage), from *frāgu, from *frēgō. Compare frain. Displaced native spörja, spörsmål.
From del, from dēl, deil, from dēl.
From time, from tími, from *tīmô, from *tī-, from *dī-.
krîch. Unlike and krig, the Swedish word has neutrum gender like kríggj.
From pod or podcast, borrowed from podcast.
From høster, haustr, from *harbustaz, *harbistaz. Ultimately from *kerp-.
sǫk, from *sakō. Cognate with sak, sak, sag, sök, sake, zaak, Saak, Sook, Sache. An unrelated word that also underwent the transformation in meaning from "legal matter" to "thing, item" is causa.
From beslut, from beslut, a besluten. 1513. Compare besluit, Beschluss.
From stellen, from stellian. Cf Stelle.
From late Old Swedish stydh, from earlier stuþ, stoþ, from stoð, stuð, from *stuþs, from *stut-. Ultimately from *stew-.
From Bild, from bilde, from biladi, bilidi. beläte.
From bilder, from bíldr, from *bīþlaz. An instrumental derivation of *bītaną. Closely related to bila.
Borrowed from critique, from criticus, from κριτικός.

From manger, margher, marghin, from mangr, margr, from *managaz.

From nær, from *nēhwiz, comparative of *nēhw. Ultimately from *h₂neḱ-.
